content: Chapter twenty two
A Prisoner
"I will ride," he said, "close up to the castle walls.
Across the promontory ran a sort of fissure, some ten yards wide and as many deep.
Several arrows had struck his armour and glanced off, and Archie now turned and quietly rode away, his horse being protected by mail like himself.
Scarce had he turned when he saw a sight which caused him for a moment to draw rein.
At full gallop the knights charged each other.
Archie received a blow from behind which struck him from his horse.
"I am Sir Archibald Forbes," Archie replied.
"It were an easy way," Archie rejoined, "to gain a possession to nominate a puppet from among the nobles already your vassals, and then to get him to do homage.
That is what Wallace and thousands of Scotchmen have fought and died to protest against, and what Scotchmen will do until their country is free."
"King Edward bids me fight in Scotland, and as his knight and vassal I put on my harness without question.
I must leave you here with the governor, for in half an hour I mount and ride away with my troop.

He will do his best to make your sojourn here easy until such time as I may have an opportunity of sending you by ship to Carlisle; and now farewell, sir," he said, giving Archie his hand, "I regret that an unkind chance has thrown so gallant a knight into my hands, and that my duty to the king forbids me from letting you go free."
"Thanks, Sir Ingram," Archie replied.
"I have ever heard of you as a brave knight, and if this misfortune must fall upon me, would sooner that I should have been captured by you than by one of less fame and honour."
The governor now had a meal with some wine set before Archie, and then left him alone.
A prodigious uproar raged in the castle.
Outside rose the war cry, "A Douglas!
A Douglas!" mingled with others of, "Glen Cairn to the rescue!" For a few minutes all was confusion, then a light suddenly burst up and grew every instant more and more bright.
"Douglas has piled faggots against the gates," Archie said to himself.
Three or four minutes later the governor with six soldiers, two of whom bore torches, entered the room.
"You must come along at once, sir knight," the governor said.
It was in vain for Archie to think of resistance, he was unarmed and helpless.
The governor unlocked a door, and with one of the torch bearers led the way down some narrow steps.
These were some fifty in number, and then a level passage ran along for some distance.
Another door was opened, and the fresh breeze blew upon them as they issued forth.
With muttered curses the men bent to their oars and every minute took them further away from Knockbawn.
Archie was bitterly disappointed.
A fortnight passed before a vessel sailed.
The winds were contrary, and the vessel was ten days upon the voyage.
The aged monarch, in the last extremity of sickness, lay upon a couch.
"I have done my utmost, sir king," Archie said firmly, "such as it was, for the freedom of my country.
No traitor am I, nor was my leader Wallace.
An involuntary murmur of admiration at his fearless bearing escaped from the knights and nobles assembled round the couch of the dying monarch.
His heart he directed should be conveyed to and deposited in the Holy Land.
Historians have striven to excuse and palliate his conduct toward Scotland.
Such is not the true function of history.
His dying wishes were not observed.
Hers grew pale.
"Bad news from home?" she almost gasped.
"What?" asked both ladies in a breath; "don't keep us in suspense."
"What? who?"
how could you?
how can we ever do without her?
"They seemed to think mine quite sufficient, Horace," replied his father, "and I'm afraid will hardly consider it necessary to ask yours."
"My son, had I refused, it would have caused her great unhappiness."
"Nor I," said Rose.
"That is an objection," replied her husband, "but if not insuperable to her, need not be to us."
"Think of your intimate friend addressing you as father!" laughed Adelaide; "it's really too ridiculous."
mr Travilla, you can't have her."
"Ah! well, I shall be quite willing to be considered a brother in law."
"Company to dinner!" shouted Horace.
"I see a carriage; don't you, papa?"
They have been late in calling."
"Some good reason for it, and they are none the less welcome," remarked Rose, quickening her pace.
"Your family has grown, Lora," remarked her brother.
"Go and speak to your uncle, Ned, Walter, Horace, and Arthur.
"Elsie, it can't be possible!" cried Carrie, the instant they found themselves alone.
Why, he's your father's friend, and nearly as old."
And you won't be taken away from us; so I give my consent."
Elsie's only answer was a mirthful, amused look.
"Oh, but I am glad to see you back!" Carrie ran on.
"Thank you.
And your husband?
what is he like?"
"That was right.
But there is the dinner bell.
I tell you, miss, I refuse my consent.
Why, it's absurd! the very idea!
"To me as much as anyone, mamma," said Elsie.
But suppose we proceed to business.
"Oh, don't talk of it yet," said Rose, with a slight tremble in her voice, and looking at Elsie's flushed, conscious face with eyes full of unshed tears.
I wasn't going to wait till they began to call me an old maid."
"Who told you there was to be a lecture?"
"Nobody, sir."
"I don't remember."
"Nor I either.
Here, take this chair beside me.
Do you know how much you are worth?"
"By no means," he said, with a slight smile of amusement at her unwonted mood.
But you belong to me still."
Please excuse my wilful misunderstanding of your query.
"My dear child!--it is fully three times that."
"Papa! is it indeed?"
I wish you to examine all the papers, certificates of stock, bonds, deeds, mortgages, and so forth."
Please excuse me.
But may I ask you a question?"
"As many as you like."
"How much are you worth?
Ah! you needn't look so quizzical.
"Something less than a million; I cannot tell you the exact number of dollars and cents."
Papa, take half of mine."
"Papa, I could never love him again, if he should object to my giving you all but a few hundred thousands."
"He would not.
And that is quite right; for he, too, is wealthy."
"What you deserve is not the question just now.
You are old enough now to do so, and I hope do not wish to shirk the responsibility, since God, in His good providence, has laid it upon you."
He spoke very gravely and Elsie's face reflected the expression of his.
"I will undertake it, asking Him for wisdom and grace to do it aright."
They were busy for the next hour or two over the papers.
"There!" cried Elsie, at length, "we have examined the last one, and I think I understand it all pretty thoroughly."
"I think you do.
"Certainly, my dear child, can you suppose I would ever think of permitting you to go alone?"
"Thank you, papa.
"Painful associations, no doubt.
"No, I'm sure not.
And now, papa, that we are done with business for the present, I have a request to make."
"Well, daughter, what is it?"
"That I should do so if possible; that meant if I could obtain your permission, papa."
"You have it.
Now about Enna?"
She doesn't deserve it from you or any one."
He drew her closer.
"Thanks, my dear, but I haven't time to sit down," she said.
Shall we go?"
"Certainly, if it suits you, Rose," replied mr Dinsmore; "and Elsie;" he added, "will you go, daughter?"
Perhaps it could be removed."
"Oh, bravely, you dear darling papa! always contriving for my enjoyment."
mr Dinsmore followed his wife from the room.
"'Twill be an early return of Carrington's call," he said, "but I have a little business with him."
"I promise to be careful to obey orders," he answered, sportively.
"Is that all?"
Now I must prepare for my ride, papa.
